content:
# Leviticus Chapter 4 — The EMET Bible (Literal)

**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 59r–59v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/LEV_004_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_004_page_002.jpg`; **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 88–89 (LXX) | `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/LEV_004_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_004_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

**1** And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying:

**2** "Speak to the sons of Israel, saying: 'A person [nefesh] when he sins unintentionally [bi-shgagah] against any of the commandments of the LORD concerning things that are not to be done, and does one of them --

**3** if the anointed priest [kohen ha-mashiach] sins, bringing guilt [le-ashmah] upon the people, then he shall bring near for his sin that he sinned a bull [par] of the herd, without blemish, to the LORD as a sin offering [chattat].

**4** And he shall bring the bull to the entrance of the tent of meeting before the LORD, and he shall lay his hand upon the head of the bull, and he shall slaughter the bull before the LORD.

**5** And the anointed priest shall take some of the blood of the bull and bring it to the tent of meeting.

**6** And the priest shall dip [ve-taval] his finger in the blood and sprinkle [ve-hizzah] some of the blood seven times before the LORD, before the veil [parokhet] of the sanctuary [ha-qodesh].

**7** And the priest shall put some of the blood upon the horns of the altar of aromatic incense before the LORD, which [is] in the tent of meeting; and all the remaining blood of the bull he shall pour out [yishpokh] at the base of the altar of the burnt offering [olah], which [is] at the entrance of the tent of meeting.

**8** And all the fat of the bull of the sin offering he shall lift off from it: the fat that covers the entrails [ha-mekhasseh al-ha-qerev] and all the fat that is upon the entrails,

**9** and the two kidneys [ha-kelayot] and the fat that is upon them, which [is] upon the flanks [al-ha-kesalim], and the appendage [ha-yoteret] upon the liver -- upon the kidneys he shall remove it --

**10** just as it is lifted off from the ox of the sacrifice of peace offerings [zevach shelamim]; and the priest shall burn them upon the altar of the burnt offering.

**11** And the hide of the bull and all its flesh, with its head and with its legs and its entrails and its dung --

**12** the whole bull he shall carry out to outside the camp, to a clean place [maqom tahor], to the ash heap [el-shefekh ha-deshen], and he shall burn it on wood with fire; upon the ash heap it shall be burned.

**13** And if the whole congregation [kol-adat] of Israel errs [yishgu] unintentionally, and the matter is hidden from the eyes of the assembly [ha-qahal], and they do any of the commandments of the LORD concerning things that are not to be done, and they incur guilt [ve-asheemu] --

**14** when the sin that they sinned against it becomes known, then the assembly shall bring near a bull of the herd as a sin offering, and they shall bring it before the tent of meeting.

**15** And the elders [ha-zeqenim] of the congregation shall lay their hands upon the head of the bull before the LORD, and he shall slaughter the bull before the LORD.

**16** And the anointed priest shall bring some of the blood of the bull to the tent of meeting,

**17** and the priest shall dip his finger in some of the blood and sprinkle [ve-hizzah] seven times before the LORD, before the veil.

**18** And some of the blood he shall put upon the horns of the altar which [is] before the LORD, which [is] in the tent of meeting; and all the remaining blood he shall pour out at the base of the altar of the burnt offering, which [is] at the entrance of the tent of meeting.

**19** And all its fat he shall lift off from it and burn upon the altar.

**20** And he shall do with the bull just as he did with the bull of the sin offering; thus shall he do with it. And the priest shall make atonement [ve-khipper] for them, and it shall be forgiven [ve-nislach] them.

**21** And he shall carry the bull outside the camp and burn it just as he burned the first bull -- it is the sin offering of the assembly.

**22** When a leader [nasi] sins and does unintentionally any of the commandments of the LORD his God concerning things that are not to be done, and he incurs guilt --

**23** or if his sin that he sinned is made known to him -- then he shall bring as his offering a male goat [se'ir izzim zachar], without blemish.

**24** And he shall lay his hand upon the head of the goat and slaughter it in the place [ba-maqom] where he slaughters the burnt offering before the LORD -- it is a sin offering.

**25** And the priest shall take some of the blood of the sin offering with his finger and put it upon the horns of the altar of the burnt offering, and its remaining blood he shall pour out at the base of the altar of the burnt offering.

**26** And all its fat he shall burn upon the altar, like the fat of the sacrifice of peace offerings; and the priest shall make atonement for him for his sin, and it shall be forgiven him.

**27** And if any person [nefesh] from the people of the land [am ha-aretz] sins unintentionally by doing any of the commandments of the LORD concerning things that are not to be done, and he incurs guilt --

**28** or if his sin that he sinned is made known to him -- then he shall bring as his offering a female goat [se'irat izzim], without blemish, for his sin that he sinned.

**29** And he shall lay his hand upon the head of the sin offering and slaughter the sin offering in the place of the burnt offering.

**30** And the priest shall take some of its blood with his finger and put it upon the horns of the altar of the burnt offering, and all its remaining blood he shall pour out at the base of the altar.

**31** And all its fat he shall remove, just as the fat is removed from the sacrifice of peace offerings, and the priest shall burn it upon the altar as a pleasing aroma [re'ach nichoach] to the LORD; and the priest shall make atonement for him, and it shall be forgiven him.

**32** And if he brings a lamb [kesev] as his offering for a sin offering, a female without blemish he shall bring it.

**33** And he shall lay his hand upon the head of the sin offering and slaughter it as a sin offering in the place where he slaughters the burnt offering.

**34** And the priest shall take some of the blood of the sin offering with his finger and put it upon the horns of the altar of the burnt offering, and all its remaining blood he shall pour out at the base of the altar.

**35** And all its fat he shall remove, just as the fat of the lamb is removed from the sacrifice of peace offerings, and the priest shall burn them upon the altar, upon the fire offerings [ishei] of the LORD; and the priest shall make atonement for him for his sin that he sinned, and it shall be forgiven him.'"

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

### Manuscript Observations (Leningrad Codex B19A, Folios 59r–59v)

1. **v.2 -- Nefesh / "a person":** The chapter opens its legal case formula with נֶפֶשׁ (nefesh), which the priestly legislation regularly uses as the subject of a culpable act: "when a person sins." Nefesh is the animating life-force, the whole-self -- body and breath together. Its use here sets the sin offering within a framework of personal responsibility: it is not a tribe, clan, or family unit but the nefesh -- the individual living person -- who incurs guilt. The subsequent four cases then demonstrate that the nefesh includes every category of person, from the anointed priest down to the common Israelite. The LXX renders ψυχή (psuche), the standard Greek equivalent for nefesh, throughout.

2. **v.2 -- Bi-shgagah / "unintentionally":** בִּשְׁגָגָה (bi-shgagah) is the foundational qualifier of the entire sin offering system of Leviticus 4. From the root sh-g-g or sh-g-h -- "to err / to wander / to go astray inadvertently" -- the word denotes a violation committed without deliberate intent: an oversight, a mistake, an inadvertent transgression. This is critically distinguished from intentional, high-handed sin (be-yad ramah, "with a raised hand," Num 15:30-31), for which no sacrificial atonement is prescribed. The sin offering applies only to unwitting violations. This distinction runs throughout Leviticus 4-5. The LXX renders ἀκουσίως (akousiōs, "involuntarily / unintentionally"), a precise rendering.

3. **v.3 -- Kohen ha-mashiach / "the anointed priest":** כֹּהֵן הַמָּשִׁיחַ (kohen ha-mashiach) is one of the theologically charged phrases of this chapter. מָשִׁיחַ (mashiach) is from the root m-sh-ch, "to anoint" -- the same root that later gives rise to the messianic title. Here it designates the high priest as the one anointed with the holy anointing oil (shemen ha-mishchah, cf. Exod 30:22-33; Lev 8:12). The anointing sets him apart as the supreme representative of Israel before the LORD. What makes the first case so severe is the phrase le-ashmah la-am -- "bringing guilt upon the people." The high priest's sin is not merely personal; by virtue of his representative office, his transgression implicates the entire community. The LXX renders ὁ ἱερεὺς ὁ χριστός (ho hiereus ho christos, "the anointed priest") -- the Greek christos is the exact equivalent of mashiach, meaning "anointed one." This is the earliest occurrence of the christos terminology in the Septuagint's Leviticus.

4. **v.3 -- Chattat / "sin offering":** חַטָּאת (chattat) appears here in its full technical cultic sense for the first time in Leviticus. The root is ch-t-' (sometimes described as "to miss the mark" or "to sin"). In the priestly legislation, chattat designates both the sin itself and the sacrifice that deals with it -- the same Hebrew word covers both concepts. This double meaning is not accidental: it captures the idea that the animal bears and carries away the sin. The LXX renders περὶ τῆς ἁμαρτίας (peri tēs hamartias, "concerning the sin") or simply ἁμαρτία (hamartia) -- using the standard Greek word for sin itself, confirming the double semantic range.

5. **v.6 -- Ve-hizzah / "and he shall sprinkle":** הִזָּה (hizzah, from n-z-h, "to sprinkle") is distinct from זָרַק (zaraq, "to dash / throw"), used in chapters 1-3. The sin offering blood rite uses hizzah -- a precise sprinkling by finger-dipping -- rather than the large-scale blood-dashing of the burnt offering and peace offering. The distinction is deliberate: zaraq describes blood thrown in quantity against the altar face; hizzah describes controlled, directional sprinkling, typically seven times. The sevenfold sprinkling before the parokhet in vv.6 and 17 is reserved for the two cases of highest status (priest and congregation) -- the individual cases of nasi and common person receive blood applied to the horns of the outer altar only. The sprinkling "before the parokhet" in the anointed priest's and congregation's cases thus penetrates deeper into the sanctuary than in the lesser cases, reflecting the greater gravity of sin by those with greater representative responsibility. The LXX renders ῥανεῖ (ranei, from rhainō, "to sprinkle"), the standard Greek equivalent for hizzah.

6. **v.6 -- Parokhet / "the veil":** פָּרֹכֶת (parokhet) is the curtain or veil that separates the Holy Place (ha-qodesh) from the Most Holy Place (qodesh ha-qodashim). It is the boundary curtain before the ark of the covenant and the kapporet (the atonement cover). Blood is sprinkled "before the parokhet" -- not through it, not upon it, but in its direction. The phrase indicates that the atonement blood is presented toward the divine throne, acknowledging that the sin has implications for the innermost sanctum. The parokhet is the boundary between the human-accessible zone and the zone of direct divine presence. The LXX renders τοῦ καταπετάσματος (tou katapetasmatos, "of the curtain / veil"), the standard Greek term (cf. Matt 27:51 for the tearing of this veil at Jesus' death).

7. **v.7 -- Altar of aromatic incense (mizbeach ha-qetoret):** The blood is applied to "the horns of the altar of aromatic incense" in the tent of meeting -- this is the gold incense altar standing before the parokhet (Exod 30:1-10). The blood application to this altar's horns is unique to the cases of the anointed priest and the congregation (vv.7, 18). The lesser cases (nasi, common person) receive blood only on the outer burnt-offering altar (vv.25, 30, 34). The incense altar stands in the Holy Place, closer to the divine presence -- blood placed there brings the atonement rite nearer to the throne.

8. **v.7 -- "He shall pour out" (yishpokh):** שָׁפַךְ (shafakh, "to pour out / spill") is used for the disposal of remaining blood at the altar base. After the prescribed blood manipulations (sprinkling before the parokhet, application to altar horns), the surplus blood is poured out at the base of the altar of burnt offering. This is not a random disposal but a deliberate drainage into the earth -- blood is life (Lev 17:11), and it returns to the ground. The LXX renders ἐκχεεῖ (ekcheei, "he shall pour out"), a close rendering.

9. **vv.8-10 -- Fat portions identical to the peace offering:** The fat portions of the sin offering bull are enumerated as identical to those of the peace offering: the covering omentum fat, kidney fat, and liver appendage. Verse 10 makes the structural point explicitly: "just as it is lifted off from the ox of the sacrifice of peace offerings." The sin offering's fat portions belong to the LORD in exactly the same way as the peace offering's. What differs is what happens to the remainder. In the peace offering, the offerer and priest share the meat. In the sin offering of the anointed priest and congregation (the two highest-rank cases), the entire carcass is carried outside the camp and burned. This total external burning is the distinctive mark of the cases where the sin affects the sanctuary itself.

10. **v.12 -- Maqom tahor / "a clean place" and shefekh ha-deshen / "the ash heap":** מָקוֹם טָהוֹר (maqom tahor, "a clean place") and שֶׁפֶךְ הַדֶּשֶׁן (shefekh ha-deshen, "the ash heap / the place of pouring out the ashes") are the two designations for the site outside the camp to which the sin offering bull is carried. This is the same location where the altar ashes are regularly deposited (Lev 1:16; 6:11). The designation maqom tahor is significant: the ash heap outside the camp is "clean" -- it is not impure even though it receives what has been removed from the sanctuary. The entire carcass (hide, flesh, head, legs, entrails, dung) is burned there. The completeness of the burning corresponds to the completeness of the priest's representative role -- nothing is left; there is no partial disposition.

11. **v.13 -- "The whole congregation" (kol-adat Yisra'el):** The second case addresses collective, corporate sin. עֵדָה (edah, "congregation / assembly") and קָהָל (qahal, "assembly") are near-synonyms here used in tandem -- v.13 mentions edah and qahal, and v.14 uses ha-qahal. The condition is that the sin is "hidden from the eyes of the assembly" (ne'elamah me-einei ha-qahal) -- the community has erred without realizing it collectively. The corporate nature of this case is matched by the corporate rite: the elders lay hands collectively (v.15), not the whole assembly one by one. The elders act as representatives of the whole. The LXX renders both edah and qahal with συναγωγή (sunagogē, "synagogue / assembly"), the term that becomes the standard word for the Jewish assembly in Greek.

12. **v.20 -- Ve-khipper / "shall make atonement" and ve-nislach / "it shall be forgiven":** The chapter's first occurrence of the atonement formula appears here at v.20 with two key verbs. כִּפֶּר (kipper, "to make atonement / to cover / to wipe clean") is the central priestly atonement verb. Its root meaning is debated: some derive it from k-p-r ("to cover"), others from the Akkadian kuppuru ("to wipe"). In the priestly context it denotes the action by which the priest, through blood manipulation, effects a state of ritual purity and restored relationship between the offerer and the LORD. The result is expressed by the passive form נִסְלַח (nislach, "it shall be forgiven / it is pardoned"), from s-l-ch, a verb used exclusively of divine forgiveness in the Hebrew Bible -- God alone is the subject of s-l-ch. The formula kipper ... nislach recurs in vv.26, 31, 35, closing each of the four cases with the same assurance. The LXX renders ἐξιλάσεται (exilāsetai, "he shall make propitiation/expiation") for kipper and ἀφεθήσεται (aphethesetai, "it shall be forgiven/released") for nislach, a close rendering of the formula.

13. **v.22 -- Nasi / "leader":** נָשִׂיא (nasi) is the leader or prince of a tribe -- in the wilderness context, each tribe's chief representative (cf. Num 1:4-16 where the twelve tribal leaders are designated). The nasi is not the high priest and not the whole congregation; he is a prominent individual whose sin has implications beyond his own household due to his representative status. The sin offering for the nasi is a male goat (se'ir izzim zachar) -- less costly than the bull prescribed for the priest and congregation. The blood rite for the nasi applies only to the horns of the outer burnt-offering altar (v.25), not to the incense altar or the parokhet. The LXX renders ἄρχων (archōn, "ruler / leader"), the standard Greek equivalent.

14. **v.23 -- Se'ir izzim zachar / "a male goat":** שְׂעִיר עִזִּים זָכָר (se'ir izzim zachar, "a male of goats") -- the sin offering animal for the nasi. Se'ir is specifically a he-goat; the term carries some semantic overlap with "hairy" (from s-'-r, "hair"), reflecting the goat's characteristic appearance. The female goat (se'irat izzim) is prescribed for the common person (v.28), creating a stepped gender/cost distinction: bull (highest rank), male goat (leader), female goat (common person), or female lamb (alternate for common person, v.32). The LXX renders χίμαρον ἐξ αἰγῶν ἄρσεν (chimaros ex aigōn arsen, "a he-goat from the goats, male"), a full rendering.

15. **v.24 -- "In the place where he slaughters the burnt offering" (ba-maqom asher yishchat et-ha-olah):** The slaughter location for the nasi's sin offering is specified as the same place where the burnt offering is slaughtered. This is the north side of the altar (cf. Lev 1:11, where the burnt offering is slaughtered "on the side of the altar northward"). The deliberate slaughter location ensures ritual propriety -- each type of offering has its proper place within the sanctuary geography.

16. **v.27 -- Am ha-aretz / "the people of the land":** עַם הָאָרֶץ (am ha-aretz, "people of the land") in this priestly context simply means the common Israelite population -- any Israelite who is not a priest and not a nasi. It is the broadest and most inclusive category. In later usage (post-exilic and Rabbinic) am ha-aretz develops negative connotations (the unlettered, the ritually lax), but here it has no pejorative sense -- it is a neutral, comprehensive designation for the ordinary Israelite populace. The LXX renders ἀπὸ τοῦ λαοῦ τῆς γῆς (apo tou laou tēs gēs, "from the people of the land"), a literal rendering.

17. **v.28 -- Se'irat izzim / "a female goat":** The common person's default sin offering is a שְׂעִירַת עִזִּים (se'irat izzim, "female of the goats") -- the feminine form of se'ir. Note the descending scale of sacrifice across the four cases: (1) anointed priest: bull; (2) congregation: bull; (3) nasi: male goat; (4) common person: female goat (or female lamb, v.32). The scale reflects the principle that the sin offering's cost is proportionate to the status and representative responsibility of the offerer, not merely to the gravity of the act.

18. **v.31 -- Re'ach nichoach / "a pleasing aroma":** The phrase רֵיחַ נִיחוֹחַ (re'ach nichoach, "a pleasing/restful aroma") appears in v.31, attached to the common person's sin offering. Notably, it does not appear in the formulas for the priest's and congregation's cases (vv.10-12, 19-21) -- those cases conclude with burning the bull outside the camp, not burning fat on the altar. The "pleasing aroma" formula reappears in the nasi's case (implied through the formula of v.26 referencing the peace offering fat) and is explicit for the common person's cases (vv.31, 35). The presence of the re'ach nichoach formula indicates the fat burning on the altar produces an aroma that "rests" (n-w-ch, same root as nichoach, and as Noah/Noach) the divine anger.

19. **vv.32-35 -- Alternate lamb option:** The chapter closes with a second provision for the common person: a female lamb (kesev, feminine) as an alternative to the female goat (v.28). The rite is identical; only the animal differs. This provision, taken together with chapter 5's further alternatives (turtle dove, pigeon, or even fine flour for the very poor), establishes the principle that access to atonement is not restricted by wealth. The priestly system builds in graduated provision so that even the poorest Israelite can bring a sin offering.

20. **v.35 -- Ishei ha-YHWH / "the fire offerings of the LORD":** The closing verse of the chapter ends with the phrase אִשֵּׁי יְהוָה (ishei YHWH, "the fire offerings of the LORD"), the same term used throughout Leviticus for the offerings consumed by fire on the altar. The chapter's final word is thus a return to the altar and its fire -- the medium through which the LORD receives what is offered. The LXX renders τὰ καρπώματα κυρίου (ta karpōmata kuriou, "the burnt-offerings of the Lord"), using the term karpōma consistently for isheh throughout Leviticus.

---

### Structural Notes

Leviticus 4 is organized as a four-case law with a descending rank structure:

**Case 1 -- The Anointed Priest (vv.3-12):**
- Offering: bull of the herd without blemish
- Blood rite: sevenfold sprinkling before the parokhet + blood on horns of incense altar + remainder at base of burnt-offering altar
- Fat: burned on the altar (same as peace offering)
- Carcass: entirely burned outside the camp at the ash heap
- Distinctive: carcass wholly removed; no "pleasing aroma" formula (the sin has defiled the sanctuary)

**Case 2 -- The Whole Congregation (vv.13-21):**
- Offering: bull of the herd (same as priest)
- Blood rite: same as Case 1 (sevenfold before parokhet, incense altar horns, base of burnt-offering altar)
- Representative rite: elders lay hands collectively
- Carcass: entirely burned outside the camp
- Atonement formula: kipper ... nislach (v.20, first occurrence)

**Case 3 -- The Nasi/Leader (vv.22-26):**
- Offering: male goat without blemish
- Blood rite: blood on horns of burnt-offering altar only + remainder at base (no parokhet sprinkling, no incense altar)
- Fat: burned on altar as peace offering fat
- Atonement formula: kipper ... nislach (v.26)

**Case 4 -- The Common Person (vv.27-35):**
- Offering: female goat (vv.27-31) OR female lamb (vv.32-35)
- Blood rite: blood on horns of burnt-offering altar only + remainder at base
- Fat: burned on altar as peace offering fat, pleasing aroma
- Atonement formula: kipper ... nislach (vv.31, 35)

The structural elegance of the chapter is visible in the blood rite gradient:

| Case | Status | Animal | Sprinkling | Altar |
|------|--------|--------|------------|-------|
| 1 | Anointed priest | Bull | 7x before parokhet | Incense altar horns |
| 2 | Congregation | Bull | 7x before parokhet | Incense altar horns |
| 3 | Nasi | Male goat | None | Burnt-offering altar horns only |
| 4 | Common person | Female goat/lamb | None | Burnt-offering altar horns only |

Cases 1 and 2 receive the full sanctuary blood rite -- sprinkling directed toward the divine inner sanctum -- because their sins carry communal or priestly weight that reaches into the heart of the sanctuary. Cases 3 and 4 receive the outer altar rite only. The depth of the blood penetration into the sanctuary corresponds precisely to the depth of the sin's corporate effect.

The fourfold repetition of the kipper ... nislach formula (vv.20, 26, 31, 35) -- once at the close of each case -- creates a rhythmic assurance: regardless of rank or social station, the unintentional sin can be atoned for, and forgiveness is the outcome. The chapter thus makes a theological claim: access to divine forgiveness through the prescribed sacrifice is universal within Israel.

---

### Vaticanus LXX Comparison Summary (pp. 88–89)

The Vaticanus pages (pa_0088–pa_0089) present the LXX text of Leviticus 4 in continuous Greek uncials across three columns. The chapter header visible on page 89 ("CAP. XXXII" or similar section marker) confirms the LXX's chapter enumeration.

Key LXX observations:

- **ὁ ἱερεὺς ὁ χριστός** (ho hiereus ho christos, "the anointed priest"): The LXX's use of christos (anointed) for kohen ha-mashiach at vv.3, 5, 16 is the earliest Septuagintal occurrence of christos in Leviticus. The theological freight of this term is heavy: in the LXX, the high priest is called "the anointed one" -- a designation that in later interpretation is refracted through the lens of Israel's messianic expectation. The Vaticanus text presents this term clearly.

- **ἀκουσίως** (akousiōs, "involuntarily"): The LXX renders bi-shgagah consistently as akousiōs throughout the chapter. This is a precise rendering: akousiōs means "against one's will / unintentionally / involuntarily," preserving the essential distinction between inadvertent and deliberate transgression.

- **ἁμαρτία** (hamartia, "sin") for chattat: The LXX uses hamartia (sin) as the rendering for both the sin itself and the sin offering. This is the same double-semantic range as the Hebrew chattat -- the offering is designated by the name of the condition it addresses. The NT's use of hamartia in passages like 2 Cor 5:21 ("he made him to be sin for us") echoes this priestly double meaning.

- **τοῦ καταπετάσματος** (tou katapetasmatos, "of the curtain/veil") for parokhet: The Vaticanus text's use of katapetasma for parokhet in vv.6, 17 uses the same word that appears in the Synoptic accounts of the Temple veil's tearing at the crucifixion (Matt 27:51; Mark 15:38; Luke 23:45), creating a direct lexical link between Leviticus 4's atonement blood rite and the NT's interpretation of Christ's death.

- **ἐξιλάσεται ... ἀφεθήσεται** for kipper ... nislach: The LXX renders the atonement formula with exilāsetai (from exilaskomai, "to propitiate / to make expiation") and aphethesetai (from aphiemi, "to release / forgive"). The word aphiemi for forgiveness is the same word Jesus uses in the Lord's Prayer ("forgive us our debts") and in the Synoptic forgiveness pronouncements, suggesting the LXX's atonement vocabulary shapes NT forgiveness language.

- **The graduated sacrifice scale**: The LXX preserves the same four-case structure with identical animal specifications: ταῦρον (tauron, "bull") for priest and congregation; χίμαρον ἄρσεν (chimaros arsen, "male he-goat") for the nasi; χίμαρον θῆλυ (chimaros thelu, "female he-goat") and ἀμνάδα (amnada, "female lamb") for the common person. The LXX Vorlage appears to agree fully with the MT on the sacrificial gradation.

— EMET
